Imtiaj.

Flash Flood Hazard Assessment & NbS Framework: Feni & Noakhali¶

GIS-Integrated Fuzzy AHP-MCDA for Climate-Resilient Disaster Management¶

Predicting vulnerability and proposing Nature-based Solutions to safeguard 1.2 million lives in the wake of the 2024 flash floods. 🌊

¶

Author: Imtiaj Iqbal Mahfuj

📝 Overview¶

This project explores spatial patterns, integrates remote sensing datasets, and applies geospatial analysis to visualize and analyze flash flood susceptibility in the Feni and Noakhali districts of Bangladesh. Following the catastrophic August 2024 flood event that affected 5.8 million people, this study aims to uncover key insights that support data-driven decision-making for urban planners and disaster management authorities.

The problem statement centers on the increasing frequency of transboundary and monsoon-driven flash floods which traditional "grey" infrastructure struggles to mitigate. By integrating a Fuzzy Analytical Hierarchy Process (FAHP) with Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA), the research overcomes the subjectivity of expert opinions to produce a highly accurate Flood Hazard Index (FHI).

The objectives were to categorize the region into five risk zones and establish a direct link between hazard mapping and implementable Nature-based Solutions (NbS). The study identifies that approximately 28% of Noakhali and 34% of Feni are at high risk, particularly impacting the Upazilas of Hatiya, Noakhali Sadar, and Chhagalnaiya.

The primary beneficiaries include regional policy-makers, environmental researchers, and local communities who can utilize these maps for better land-use planning and climate adaptation. The summary of the findings highlights Hatiya Island as a "Very High" hazard zone and proposes ecosystem-based strategies like mangrove restoration and floating agriculture to build long-term resilience.

🛠️ Tools & Technologies¶

⚙️ Methodology¶

Step Description
1. Data Collection Acquired USGS DEM (Elevation), BMD Rainfall data, BWDB Hydrographic maps, SRDI Soil data, and OSM Road networks.
2. Preprocessing Standardized all layers to WGS 84 (UTM Zone 46N), clipped to study area, and resampled to 30m spatial resolution.
3. Analysis Applied FAHP to calculate weights for 7 parameters. Performed Weighted Overlay Analysis in ArcGIS Pro to generate the Flood Hazard Index.
4. Visualization Classified results using Natural Breaks (Jenks) and validated against Sentinel-1 SAR imagery from the August 2024 floods.

Graphical Methodology

Study Area (Noakhali & Feni)

Spatial Distributions of the Flood Hazard Factors & Flood Hazard Map

Pairwise comparison matrix for flood hazard contributing factors

¶

📊 Results & Insights¶

  • 🔹 High-Risk Hotspots: Hatiya Island is identified as a "Very High" hazard area due to its low elevation (18-32m) and exposure to tidal surges.
  • 🔹 District Vulnerability: Noakhali Sadar and Chhagalnaiya are classified as "High" hazard zones, while Begumganj falls under "Moderate" risk.
  • 🔹 Influential Factors: Elevation and Rainfall intensity were found to be the most critical contributing factors to flash flood susceptibility in this deltaic region.

Caption: Spatial distribution of flood hazard zones.

❓ Why this project?¶

  • The Implementation Gap: Existing literature often focuses on mapping without providing site-specific, actionable adaptation strategies. This project bridges that gap by proposing NbS.
  • Uncertainty Management: Standard AHP struggles with human subjectivity; the use of Fuzzy Logic ensures more robust and reliable parameter weighting for natural phenomena.
  • Climate Action: Directly aligns with SDG 13, offering a proactive ecosystem-based resilience framework rather than reactive engineering solutions.

✨ Features¶

  • FAHP-GIS Model: A replicable and scalable model for multi-criteria disaster assessment.
  • NbS Toolbox: Recommendations for wetland restoration, afforestation, and floating agriculture.
  • Validation-Backed: Model results are validated against real-world historical flood data (August 2024).
  • Exportable Assets: High-resolution GeoTIFF hazard maps and thematic vector layers.
